# Retrospective Meeting Template: 5 Formats That Work (2026)

## Summary

Published 2026-05-07 — one of the most recently published sources in the sweep. MeetGeek's guide covers five core formats with an important thesis: "Most retros don't fail at the format. They fail at the commitment step." The format is the container; the commitment step (owner, date, tracker) is what determines whether anything changes.

The five formats covered with when-NOT-to-use guidance (which is unusually useful):

**Start / Stop / Continue** - Best for new teams or after drama. NOT recommended when team is stuck in repetition (easy to fill with the same items every sprint).

**4Ls (Liked / Learned / Lacked / Longed For)** - Best for end of project phase or quarter, surfaces career signal not just process friction. NOT for routine mid-sprint retros (takes emotional energy that doesn't recharge every two weeks).

**Mad / Sad / Glad** - Best for teams coming off a hard sprint or incident. Permission to name frustration directly.

**Sailboat** - Best for visual thinkers or teams needing to discuss strategic direction, not just the last sprint.

**DAKI (Drop / Add / Keep / Improve)** - Best for mature teams; forces commitment in a way Start/Stop/Continue doesn't. NOT for teams that haven't agreed on core practices.

Format rotation across a quarter (explicit quarterly cadence example provided).

Commitment step protocol: before anyone leaves — (1) cap total actions at 3, (2) assign a name to each, (3) set a date, (4) add to a tracker. "The format you pick matters less than whether you actually run that step."

Facilitation notes on manager-as-facilitator anti-pattern: "The worst facilitator is a manager who sits in the retro every sprint. The team can't name manager-related problems."

## Key quotations / statistics

- "Most retros don't fail at the format. They fail at the commitment step — the one minute at the end where every decision gets a name, a date, and a tracker."
- "The format you pick matters less than whether you actually run that step."
- "The worst facilitator is a manager who sits in the retro every sprint. The team can't name manager-related problems."
- "A team that only ever runs Start/Stop/Continue sees 30% of what's there. Scrum teams working inside SAFe, LeSS, Scrum@Scale, or Kanban should also rotate at the program level."
- On DAKI: "You're not just starting something, you're adding or dropping it." — sharper accountability than Start/Stop/Continue.

## Annotations for stinger-forge

- The "when NOT to use" guidance for each format is gold for `guides/01-formats.md`. Most format references only say when to use; this source explicitly names misuse cases.
- The commitment step framing is the philosophical anchor for `guides/00-principles.md` — the retro is measured by what the team does differently, not by the quality of the discussion.
- The manager-as-facilitator anti-pattern belongs in `guides/03-facilitation.md` as a critical warning.
- The quarterly rotation example (with explicit sprint-by-sprint table) is directly usable as a template in `guides/01-formats.md`.
- Published 2026-05-07 — this is the most current source in the sweep and should be flagged as authoritative for 2026.
